The variance there is based on different tiers that relate to how much volume or which channels we're focusing on.
What I can tell you is in our next conversation, I will bring you multiple options that project different ROIs, right?
So what does a $12,000 a month investment look like versus a $30,000 a month investment?
And I know right now you said you're spending, you know, the $5,000 to $10,000 range.
So we might want to, you know, anchor around that number and look at the $12,000 a month commitment.
But my point here is that we're going to come in with, you know, multiple tiers and give, you know, give you the option and see the trade-offs between them.
Here's some, I think I follow, you know, where it starts, but, you know, we're kind of, I imagine, of course, we need to start with strategy, but.
So I guess I'm a little worried.
We want to move fast and get results.

I guess I'm a little worried, you know, on just how all this, like, I think I generally follow some of how you shared it, but, you know, we really need to make sure we're getting ROI as fast as possible.
Absolutely.
So what I can tell you is that majority of our client, of our customers are able to see that ROI in as little as eight weeks.

So we're talking two month turnarounds here to go from, you know, not having necessarily a fully fleshed out strategy to having a fully fleshed out strategy, having an engine built where we're already producing artifacts and showing that increase in output.
And then past that two months, really, there's all we're doing from there is further optimizing and testing.
So within, like I said, six to eight weeks, you're going to see a huge difference in the strategy and the outputs.
And then past there, we are just going to get more data, more insight into change.

So what I can tell you is that relative to other options like onboarding with an agency, integrating with their organization, or going through the procurement process of a lot of other tools and training the team, two months is a blip relative to how long those take.
So I think if you and your team would feel comfortable with that sort of timeline, there's a lot of value that we could demonstrate in that short time.
All right, let's wrap up because we only have time.
Yeah, let's give you time for questions.
Anything you want to wrap up with?
Oh, perfect.
There we go.
Yeah, we'll just talk about next steps.
I know we're short on time here.

So just to give you a sense of what's coming next, assuming that there is a good mutual fit today that you guys are feeling good about what you've heard and are willing to meet with us again, tell us a little bit more about your business and see a further proposal and also a demo.
We would love to get that on the calendar for 8-11 so we can give you our initial thought.
From there, we would love to get you into that strategy sprint.
And just to maintain quality, we only onboard two clients per week, and we're fully booked for August.
So that's why that 9-1 is the soonest date we have available.
And then by 10-15, we can have that growth engine launched.
So that just gives you a sense of time.
So I know we got to jump here.
I really appreciate the time today, everybody.
And I will certainly, I will be actually putting that formal proposal on your calendar just to kind of hold the time there, Kyle.
And I'll work with you to dial that in if you guys need some more time or want to even move that up a little bit.
So thank you guys for the time today.
Cool.
And scene.
And scene.
All right.
Thank you.
How did you find it?
I found it, yeah, like I said, good.
I had a lot of fun with the exercise.
I think probably like one of the biggest challenges for me is that I felt like I needed to do a lot of work in organizing the value props and pain points that aren't as.
You know, available on the website, maybe relative to like companies that are a little bit further along.
So I definitely had some, you know, fun there and I enjoy doing it.
But yeah, it was certainly a challenge, but I'm game for it.
What did you guys think?
I'm very curious, very eager to hear feedback.
Okay, I wrote three main things down.
I think, okay, the discovery was great.
A little long, perhaps, could have just sped through it a bit faster, but I don't know.
You can kind of tell.
It depends on people's reactions.
I know we weren't giving you too much.
I think I would have asked more pointed questions in the pitch part.
Like, instead of just like, do you guys have questions on this slide?
Like, have some pre-planned, like, questions where you can almost, like, catch them, admitting what you just said.
So that was the main, like, the main constructive thing.
I really liked how before you showed pricing, you asked how much you're spending.
That gives you, like, major leverage, usually.
And then I thought the next steps were very, like, clear to the point, And, like, you brought in the FOMO of, like, we're booked.
Get on the train or get out kind of thing.
Anyway, those are my notes.
And what else others have?
I think the end was good.
I think, like, just the way you presented makes me think about, like, the information on the website and how that needs a shitload of work.
So that was good that I'm here for that.
But I thought, yeah, I thought the FOMO was good.
I think for what you had, you know, how comfortable you felt with talking about what we do is good.

I think the, like, presentation itself, at least for me, I guess it depends who you're talking to, but I feel like, at least for, like, decision maker, you know, like, CMO level people, probably, like, going through the whole deck is unnecessary.
It's, like, you've done discovery to know what our questions are.
Just focus on that.
And if there's something you think...
know, it's it's know is.
I It you.
Can benefit, okay, do that too, but like, I think going through the whole deck from beginning to end, probably not totally necessary, you know, depending on who's on the call, I guess.

And then, yeah, and then the other part was like, I feel like there's definitely questions you just would not know the answers to, and I think it's maybe looking for like, do you have a good way of kind of keeping the interest, but then kind of deflecting like, you know, like obviously like you're not going to be the person doing delivery, but like we have people on the team that can't answer those questions.
And it's like, how do you juggle that?
Because I was looking for a little bit, but yeah, we're all pretty good with the materials.
I don't know what we provide you, but if it was just the website, I feel like that was pretty good.
Nothing.
Yeah, literally just the website.
Yeah, plus one to a lot of that was good.
I mean, you know, a variety of things I can nitpick in addition, but like that stuff's like most, all coachable things.
So just giving you context of, you know, in this.
Thank

Situation, or this is how we approach it, and, you know, I think, you know, the somewhat related to kind of what Jason was mentioning, I do think is, is useful of, like, not jumping to answer is something that I think can be helpful at times, like getting more context in these conversations, and as well as, like, maybe even, maybe not as applicable here, but at times as we're kind of, like, going through that motion oftentimes, like, one thing I noticed Marcel does quite well, is, like, challenge assumptions as well, and, like, you know, finding those opportunities and not letting us drive is what we think is possible, because there's this blend of, again, this maybe, like, relates to the point Jason was talking about, of, like, the slides and things of that nature, where we're trying to keep this, like, casualness, consultative, you know, nature to it a bit, and I think naturally that's going to have to hold it off.
As we continue to grow and bring other expertises into the mix and everyone's going to have their own vibe and we'll experiment.
But, you know, I sometimes wonder if we go like to conventional sales and pitchy, like how that's going to work for us and how we work.
And so, you know, sometimes slides for me is like kind of triggering to that.
I think we've all been in meetings, right, where we're like getting slides to death.
And, you know, we found some value in not doing that.
But I think we've even gone too extreme on that side and how we operate.
If anything, our slides can be a whole lot better.
They're pretty bad.
Totally.
And I think just to respond to like quantity of slides, I am a fewer is better actual pitching environment less.
In this instance for the role play, because I just still have such a very basic understanding of how things actually work.
I think I created more content than I typically.
I in a discovery session like this to try to sandbox in the world we're in for the role play.
Because I have been in role plays before where you don't have visuals, you don't have maybe like enough context, and it can quickly kind of go off the rails.
So the deck is in a way is like my way of trying to constrain and control the conversation.
But totally hear you.
think the best practice is less slides, less information, be an expert, teach them, challenge them.
But sometimes, you know, that can just manifest a little bit differently in that.
Yeah, totally.
And I thought you were thoughtful about, you know, anticipating, you know, potential points of friction and trying to address those proactively.
You know, I think that's a thing we can certainly do better at and like working into our mix.
try to cram so much into optimizing to the least amount of calls possible that sometimes we're making certain trade-offs.
So oftentimes I feel like when I go through this, it's like kind of like, oh, yeah.
Should experiment bring that, you know, that is a good best practice.
That we're not like nailing today.
And so very much just trying to, you know, keep a, I guess the marketer and be like, think from an experimentation basis and, you know, see if some of this stuff helps us be more effective.
Because sometimes we just go through and we jump in so quick, wanting to show how we work and jump into the platform and show all these cool things.
And, you know, I've thought back, like we have one of these recently, I was on with Marcel.
I thought back and I was like, oh man, they brought up this good point.
We just like dove right past it and like, had we just like really double clicked on it, that could have really informed a lot of like where the conversation goes.
So anyway, so I thought you did a good job being thoughtful and trying to anticipate some of that and anchor us on some key points.
Thank you.
Any final question?
Yeah, I mean, I'd be super curious to actually see like what your intro discovery deck looks like.
I would love, love to, love to see that if possible.
Just kind of line it up.
I've kind of wow you.
It's missing so much.
Like, literally what we do is, like, we have just a variety of different slides we've tweaked over time, and we'll pop into a few here and there.
We may show two slides in the intro.
Sometimes more.
Sometimes it just depends.

Occasionally we'll get on these calls where, like, it's a little tough and annoying, where it's just, like, this actually may speak to, like, some of the stuff we even talk about the website or whatever.
But they're just, like, oh, you do growth.
You do all the things growth.
Oh, AI is amazing.
Can you do this?
Can you do that?
And so sometimes we have to, like, go through a little bit more, like, expectation setting.
But that's, luckily it doesn't happen a ton.
And most of the time it's kind of, like, you know, flashing a couple slides that talk about our work streams and then, like, how we structure sprints and execution phase.
And then we dive right into, like, this is how we work, this is how we think, here's the platform and, you know, kind of really get into.

But, like, I do think there's some, like, notable missing things of, like, structuring, like, you know, we've been talking a lot about, you know, needing to own the narrative around GEO and, you know, what's happening and, you know, broadly and how we think about that.

And I think there's just opportunity for us to find ways to, you know, do a little bit more upfront education and, like, you know, add a little bit of, you know, wow factor to, like, okay, these guys are thinking a bit differently before we jump into, like, how we actually work.
Totally.
Yeah, that was actually one of the things I was spending a lot of time thinking about, kind of torn over, was whether or not to include, like, GEO as, like, or all of those services.
So I think oftentimes when you can, when you start slapping a bunch of services, you get away from, like, what makes you feel like you do and can be very tactical.
So that's a challenge.
I was also seeing.
Visualizing what we do, GrowthX does, is also another area that was difficult to do.
And for me, at least, if I'm imagining myself as the level of detail that I got for the message from the VP of Growth and the prompt is just like, hey, I'm just interested in growth and AI.
I think there's still a lot of education that needs to be done for that person of what we do, how it works.
So, yeah, lots of room for improvement.
Cool.
Well, appreciate you taking all the time to put that together and being so thoughtful about it.
Absolutely.
And thank you guys for being flexible at the schedule and being good scene partners today.
You got it.
Thank you, Kyle.
Thank you, guys.
All right.
See you, bud.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Panel interview and sales role play with Kyle Rios-Merwin for Account Executive position at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=86.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Kyle demonstrated strong preparation and product knowledge, creating a detailed pitch deck and discovery process based solely on GrowthX's website](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=304.0)
  - [The role play focused on GrowthX's AI-powered content creation services, with Kyle pitching to a fictional Apollo.io team](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Feedback highlighted areas for improvement: shorter discovery, more pointed questions, and less reliance on slides for expert-level conversations](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=1501.0)

## Topics

### Role Play Setup

  - [Kyle pitched GrowthX services to a fictional Apollo.io team (Kyle G as VP Growth, Jason as Content Lead, Bridget as CFO)](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=484.0)
  - [Scenario: Apollo looking to expand content and AI capabilities as they transition to enterprise-level company](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Kyle created detailed context and pitch deck based solely on GrowthX website information](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)

### Discovery Process

  - [Kyle conducted thorough discovery, asking about current challenges, urgency, and existing solutions](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=488.0)
  - [Identified key pain points: scaling content production, maintaining quality, increasing velocity, and operationalizing AI](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Slightly long discovery phase noted in feedback, suggestion to speed up based on prospect reactions](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=762.0)

### GrowthX Value Proposition

  - [Presented GrowthX as expert-led, AI-powered growth solution combining services and software](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=1418.0)
  - [Highlighted differentiators: scalable content production, expert execution, fast implementation, and always-current technology](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=1429.0)
  - [Showcased case studies (DeepGram, Ramp, Reddit) demonstrating significant improvements in organic growth, content output, and velocity](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=1701.0)

### Pricing and Next Steps

  - [Transparent pricing model: $20k one-time strategy sprint, $12k-$60k monthly for AI growth engine](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2024.0)
  - [Emphasized quick ROI timeline (8 weeks) and fully managed solution](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2117.0)
  - [Created urgency with limited onboarding slots and potential September 1st start date](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2225.0)

### Feedback and Areas for Improvement

  - [Reduce reliance on slides, especially for executive-level conversations](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2561.0)
  - [Prepare more pointed questions to guide prospects to desired conclusions](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2707.0)
  - [Improve deflection techniques for questions outside immediate expertise](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2431.0)
  - [Consider challenging prospect assumptions more directly](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2527.0)
  - [Balance between casual consultative approach and traditional sales tactics](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2539.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Kyle to review GrowthX's actual discovery deck and process](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2691.0)
  - [GrowthX team to consider improvements to website content and sales materials based on role play insights](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2822.0)
  - [Explore ways to better educate prospects on GrowthX's unique approach and differentiation in the market](https://fathom.video/share/ncNzzqK3pJU2sabTkTXkYMJxs-uvoFSq?tab=summary&timestamp=2828.0)

action_items.description: Prep comprehensive proposal for Apollo team. Incl multiple pricing tiers ($12k-$60k/mo).  Intro growth expert w/ industry expertise. Schedule next mtg for 8-11.
